q: Which of the following best distinguishes Object-Oriented Programming from Procedural Programming?
options: OOP uses functions while procedural uses methods
OOP organizes code around data (objects) while procedural organizes around functions/procedures
OOP cannot use loops while procedural can
OOP is only used for web development
explanation: OOP organizes a program as a collection of objects that combine data and behavior. Procedural programming organizes code as a sequence of functions/procedures that operate on separate data. This is the fundamental distinction.

q: A class in OOP is best described as:
options: A blueprint or template that defines the properties and behaviors of objects
A reserved memory location for data storage
explanation: A class is a blueprint/template defining attributes (data) and methods (behavior). An object is an instance of a class. Confusing class with object is a common IBPS trap.

q: Which OOP concept refers to bundling data and the methods that operate on that data within a single unit, and restricting direct access to some components?
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data and methods together and hides internal state using access modifiers (private, protected). Abstraction hides complexity â€” these two are often confused in exams.

q: What is an object in OOP?
options: A data type defined by the programmer
A specific instance of a class with actual values assigned to its attributes
A method that returns a value
explanation: An object is a runtime instance of a class. When a class is instantiated, memory is allocated and attribute values are assigned, creating an object.

q: Abstraction in OOP means:
options: Hiding the implementation details and showing only the essential features to the user
Copying the properties of one class to another
Allowing multiple forms of a method
Restricting access to class members using private keyword
explanation: Abstraction exposes only what is necessary and hides internal complexity. Note: Encapsulation is about bundling + hiding data; Abstraction is about hiding implementation complexity. These are frequently confused.

q: Which of the following is NOT a primary pillar of OOP?
explanation: The four pillars of OOP are Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism. Compilation is a language-processing step, not an OOP concept.

q: Inheritance in OOP allows:
options: An object to take multiple forms
A class to acquire properties and behaviors of another class
Data to be hidden from outside classes
A method to be defined without implementation
explanation: Inheritance enables a child/derived class to inherit attributes and methods from a parent/base class, promoting code reusability. Polymorphism allows multiple forms â€” a common confusion.

q: Polymorphism in OOP refers to:
options: Wrapping data and functions into a single unit
The ability of a single interface or method to work with different data types or take multiple forms
Inheriting properties from a parent class
Creating multiple objects from the same class
explanation: Polymorphism (many forms) allows the same method name or interface to behave differently based on the object or context. It includes method overloading (compile-time) and overriding (runtime).

q: What is instantiation in OOP?
options: Defining a class with attributes and methods
The process of creating an object from a class
Inheriting methods from a parent class
Overriding a method in a subclass
explanation: Instantiation is the process of creating a specific instance (object) from a class definition. For example, 'Dog d = new Dog();' instantiates the Dog class.

q: In OOP, 'message passing' refers to:
options: Sending emails between users of an application
The mechanism by which objects communicate by invoking each other's methods
Transferring data between classes using inheritance
explanation: Message passing is how objects interact â€” one object sends a message (calls a method) to another object to request an action. It is the primary communication mechanism in OOP.

q: Which of the following statements about a class and an object is CORRECT?
options: A class is an instance of an object
Multiple classes can be created from a single object
An object is an instance of a class
A class and an object are identical concepts
explanation: An object is an instance of a class. A class is the template; objects are actual instances. This reversal (class is instance of object) is a classic IBPS trap.

q: Object identity in OOP means:
options: Every object has a name assigned by the programmer
Each object has a unique identity that distinguishes it from other objects, even if they have identical attribute values
The class name serves as the identity of its objects
Objects sharing the same state have the same identity
explanation: Object identity is the property that distinguishes one object from another even when their states are identical. Two objects with the same attribute values are still different objects with distinct identities (memory addresses).

q: Which OOP principle is best illustrated by an ATM machine where users interact with a simple interface without knowing the internal banking logic?
explanation: Abstraction hides the complex implementation (banking logic, network calls) and presents a simple interface to the user. This is a classic real-world example of abstraction.

q: In procedural programming, which of the following is the primary unit of modularization?
explanation: In procedural programming (e.g., C), the function or procedure is the primary unit of code organization. In OOP, the object (and class) is the primary unit.

q: Which access modifier in OOP implements encapsulation most strictly by preventing access from outside the class?
explanation: The 'private' access modifier restricts access to only within the class itself, providing the strictest encapsulation. 'public' allows access from anywhere; 'protected' allows access within the package and subclasses.

q: A child class that inherits from a parent class is also called:
explanation: The class that inherits is called the derived class or subclass. The class being inherited from is the base class or superclass. These naming conventions are frequently tested.

q: Which type of polymorphism is resolved at compile time?
explanation: Method overloading (same method name, different parameters) is resolved at compile time â€” called compile-time or static polymorphism. Method overriding (same signature in subclass) is resolved at runtime â€” dynamic polymorphism.

q: Which of the following real-world analogies best represents the relationship between a class and an object?
options: A cookie cutter (class) and the cookies made from it (objects)
A recipe ingredient and the chef
A programming language and its compiler
explanation: A cookie cutter is the template (class) and each cookie produced is an instance (object). This is the standard analogy â€” a class is the mold/blueprint, objects are the actual instances created from it.

q: In OOP, which of the following benefits does inheritance primarily provide?
explanation: Inheritance promotes code reusability by allowing a subclass to reuse attributes and methods of a superclass without rewriting them. Data hiding is encapsulation; multiple forms is polymorphism.

q: What is the term for the special method that is automatically called when an object is created from a class?
explanation: A constructor is a special method with the same name as the class that is automatically invoked during object instantiation to initialize the object's attributes.

q: Which OOP concept allows a subclass to provide a specific implementation of a method already defined in its superclass?
explanation: Method overriding allows a subclass to redefine a method from the superclass with the same name and signature, enabling runtime polymorphism. Overloading is having multiple methods with the same name but different parameters.

q: In OOP terminology, what does 'state' of an object refer to?
options: The class from which the object was created
The set of values currently held by the object's attributes/fields
The methods defined in the object's class
The memory address of the object
explanation: The state of an object is represented by the current values of its instance variables/attributes. The class defines what attributes exist; the object's state is the actual values stored in those attributes.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of OOP over procedural programming?
options: OOP programs always execute faster than procedural programs
OOP makes it easier to model real-world entities and manage complexity through modular design
OOP uses less memory than procedural programming
explanation: OOP's primary advantage over procedural programming is its ability to model real-world entities as objects, improving code organization, reusability, maintainability, and scalability. Performance is not inherently better.

q: An abstract class in OOP:
options: Can be instantiated directly to create objects
Cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods without implementation
explanation: An abstract class cannot be directly instantiated. It may contain abstract methods (without body) that must be implemented by concrete subclasses. It can also have concrete methods, unlike interfaces (in traditional OOP).

q: Which of the following correctly describes 'behavior' of an object in OOP?
options: The data values stored in the object's fields
The unique identifier of the object in memory
The actions an object can perform, defined by its methods
The class hierarchy the object belongs to
explanation: An object has three characteristics: state (attribute values), behavior (methods/actions it can perform), and identity (unique reference). Behavior is defined by the methods declared in the class.

q: Which of the following is an example of runtime polymorphism?
options: Two methods with the same name but different number of parameters in the same class
A subclass overriding a method from the superclass, resolved via dynamic dispatch at runtime
A constructor with multiple parameter lists
Using a variable to store different data types
explanation: Runtime polymorphism occurs through method overriding where the actual method called is determined at runtime based on the actual object type, not the reference type. Overloading is compile-time polymorphism.

q: In OOP, which of the following best defines 'encapsulation'?
options: The ability to inherit features from multiple parent classes
The mechanism of hiding internal object details and exposing only necessary interfaces
The process of creating objects from abstract classes
Resolving method calls at compile time
explanation: Encapsulation is the mechanism of wrapping data and methods together in a class, and restricting direct access to some components via access modifiers. It protects object integrity by preventing unintended external interference.

q: When we say two objects are 'equal' in OOP, it could mean they have the same state. However, object identity means:
options: Objects with same state are always the same object
Two objects can have the same state but still be distinct objects with different identities
Object identity is determined by the class name
Identity and equality are the same concept in OOP
explanation: Identity vs equality is a key OOP concept. Two objects may have identical attribute values (equal state) but are still different objects with different memory addresses (different identities). In Java, == checks identity; .equals() checks state equality.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about procedural programming compared to OOP?
options: Procedural programming naturally models real-world entities better than OOP
In procedural programming, data and functions are separate and not inherently tied together
Procedural programs are harder to write than OOP programs
explanation: In procedural programming (like C), data structures and functions are separate entities. OOP binds data and functions together in objects, leading to better real-world modeling and data protection.

q: The 'new' keyword in Java/C++ during object creation is used to:
options: Allocate memory and instantiate an object from a class
explanation: The 'new' keyword allocates heap memory for a new object and calls the class constructor to initialize it. This is the instantiation process: memory allocation + constructor invocation.

q: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates 'message passing' in OOP?
options: A variable storing a string value
One object calling a method on another object to request an operation
A subclass inheriting a method from a superclass
explanation: Message passing is the mechanism by which objects interact. When object A calls a method on object B, A is 'sending a message' to B requesting an action. It is the OOP equivalent of function calls in procedural programming.

q: Which type of inheritance involves a class inheriting from more than one parent class?
explanation: Multiple inheritance is when a class inherits from two or more parent classes. Java does not support multiple inheritance through classes (to avoid the 'diamond problem') but supports it through interfaces.

q: What is the 'diamond problem' in OOP?
options: When a class has too many attributes
Ambiguity arising when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from the same base class
When encapsulation prevents access to required data
explanation: The diamond problem occurs in multiple inheritance when class D inherits from B and C, both of which inherit from A. It creates ambiguity about which version of A's methods D should use. Java resolves this by disallowing multiple class inheritance.

q: In OOP, a class that cannot have any subclasses is declared as:
explanation: A class declared as 'final' cannot be extended (subclassed). This is used when a class should not be modified through inheritance, such as the String class in Java.

q: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of encapsulation?
options: Flexibility to change internal implementation without affecting external code
Controlled access to object data through getters and setters
explanation: Code reusability through inheritance is a benefit of inheritance, not encapsulation. Encapsulation's benefits include data hiding, controlled access, flexibility to change internals, and improved maintainability.

q: In OOP, the term 'superclass' refers to:
options: A class that has been optimized for performance
The parent class from which another class inherits
A class with more than five methods
The main class that contains the entry point of a program
explanation: Superclass (also base class or parent class) is the class being inherited from. The class that inherits is the subclass (child class or derived class). These terms are used interchangeably in exams.

q: Which of the following programming languages is primarily procedural (not object-oriented)?
explanation: C is a procedural programming language. Java, Python, and C++ are primarily object-oriented (C++ is multi-paradigm but emphasizes OOP). This is a direct factual question often tested in IBPS SO.

q: An interface in OOP (as in Java) is best described as:
options: A class with only private methods
A contract specifying method signatures that implementing classes must provide
A type of constructor for abstract classes
A class that can be instantiated directly
explanation: An interface defines a contract â€” a set of method signatures (and constants) that any implementing class must fulfill. Interfaces support abstraction and multiple inheritance in Java. They cannot be instantiated.

q: Which of the following is the correct order of steps when instantiating an object?
options: Declare reference â†’ define class â†’ allocate memory â†’ call constructor
Define class â†’ declare reference variable â†’ allocate memory (new) â†’ constructor initializes object
Call constructor â†’ allocate memory â†’ define class â†’ declare reference
Allocate memory â†’ define class â†’ call constructor â†’ declare reference
explanation: Correct sequence: First the class is defined (blueprint). Then a reference variable is declared. 'new' allocates memory. Finally the constructor initializes the object's state. E.g., 'Dog d = new Dog();'

q: Which OOP concept is violated if a class exposes all its internal data fields as public?
explanation: Making all fields public violates encapsulation because it removes data hiding and allows unrestricted external access and modification of object state. Proper encapsulation uses private fields with controlled public accessors.

q: What distinguishes an instance variable from a class (static) variable in OOP?
options: Instance variables are shared across all objects; class variables are unique per object
Instance variables are unique to each object; class variables are shared across all instances of the class
Instance variables must be initialized in the constructor; class variables cannot
explanation: Instance variables hold unique values for each object (separate copy per instance). Class/static variables are shared among all instances of the class â€” there is only one copy regardless of how many objects exist.

q: In OOP, 'late binding' or 'dynamic binding' is associated with:
options: Method overloading resolved at compile time
Method overriding resolved at runtime based on the actual object type
explanation: Late/dynamic binding means the method to be invoked is determined at runtime based on the actual object type, not the reference type. This is the mechanism behind runtime polymorphism through method overriding.

q: Which of the following is a key difference between abstraction and encapsulation?
options: Abstraction hides implementation details at the design level; encapsulation hides data at the implementation level using access modifiers
Abstraction uses private keyword; encapsulation uses abstract keyword
Both are identical concepts with different names
Encapsulation is for inheritance; abstraction is for polymorphism
explanation: Abstraction is a design concept â€” hiding 'what' the implementation does internally (showing only essential features). Encapsulation is an implementation mechanism â€” hiding 'how' using access modifiers to protect data. They are related but distinct.

q: A class 'Vehicle' is the parent of classes 'Car' and 'Truck'. This is an example of:
explanation: Hierarchical inheritance is when multiple subclasses inherit from a single parent class (one parent â†’ many children). Multiple inheritance is one child with multiple parents. Multilevel is a chain (Aâ†’Bâ†’C).

q: Consider: class A â†’ class B â†’ class C (C inherits from B, B inherits from A). This is:
explanation: Multilevel inheritance forms a chain where a class inherits from another derived class. Here C is derived from B which is derived from A â€” forming a three-level inheritance chain.

q: Which of the following statements about constructors is INCORRECT?
options: A constructor has the same name as the class
A constructor can be overloaded with different parameter lists
A constructor must explicitly return a value using the return statement
A constructor is called automatically when an object is created
explanation: Constructors do NOT have a return type and do not use a return statement to return a value. They share the class name, can be overloaded, and are called automatically at instantiation. The 'must return a value' option is the trap.

q: In OOP, which concept supports the principle 'Program to an interface, not an implementation'?
explanation: 'Program to an interface' means using abstract types/interfaces in code rather than concrete classes. This leverages abstraction (defining contracts) and polymorphism (different implementations can be swapped). It promotes flexibility and loose coupling.

q: Which of the following best describes the 'IS-A' relationship in OOP?
options: Composition â€” one class contains an object of another class
Inheritance â€” a subclass IS-A type of its superclass (e.g., Dog IS-A Animal)
Encapsulation â€” data is wrapped inside a class
Association â€” two classes use each other
explanation: IS-A represents inheritance. If Dog inherits from Animal, then 'Dog IS-A Animal' is true. The 'HAS-A' relationship represents composition. IBPS exams test whether candidates can distinguish IS-A (inheritance) from HAS-A (composition).

q: Which of the following is TRUE about objects of the same class?
options: All objects of the same class share the same state
All objects of the same class share the same methods but have their own separate instance variables
Objects of the same class cannot have different attribute values
Two objects of the same class always have the same identity
explanation: Objects of the same class share the same method definitions (stored in the class), but each object has its own copy of instance variables with independent values. Methods are not duplicated per object â€” they are shared.

q: In OOP, which of the following terms refers to the process by which an object determines which method to call based on the actual type of the object at runtime?
explanation: Dynamic dispatch (also called dynamic method lookup or late binding) is the mechanism by which the correct overridden method is selected at runtime based on the actual object type. It is the foundation of runtime polymorphism in OOP.

q: Which constructor is automatically called when an object is created without any arguments?
explanation: A default constructor takes no arguments and is automatically invoked when an object is instantiated without passing any parameters.

q: What happens if a class defines at least one parameterized constructor and no default constructor?
options: Objects can still be created without arguments
Creating an object without arguments causes a compile error
The parameterized constructor acts as the default
explanation: Once any user-defined constructor is provided, the compiler no longer auto-generates a default constructor. Attempting to create an object without arguments results in a compile-time error.

q: A constructor that accepts another object of the same class as a parameter is called a:
explanation: A copy constructor takes a reference (usually const reference) to an object of the same class and initializes the new object as a copy of the passed object.

q: What is the correct signature of a copy constructor for class `MyClass`?
explanation: The copy constructor must take a const reference to the same class: `MyClass(const MyClass& obj)`. Passing by value would cause infinite recursion.

q: Which of the following statements about destructors is TRUE?
options: A class can have multiple destructors
A destructor is called automatically when an object goes out of scope
A destructor can return a value
explanation: A destructor is automatically invoked when an object's lifetime ends (goes out of scope or is deleted). It has no parameters and no return type, and a class can have only one destructor.

q: What is the correct syntax for declaring a destructor for class `Sample`?
explanation: A destructor is declared with a tilde (~) followed by the class name, no return type, and no parameters: `~Sample()`.

q: The `this` pointer in C++ refers to:
options: The base class of the current object
The address of the current object
A pointer to the class definition
The first member of the class
explanation: `this` is an implicit pointer available inside non-static member functions that holds the address of the object on which the member function is called.

q: Which of the following is a valid use of the `this` pointer?
options: To distinguish between member variables and local variables with the same name
To access private members of another class
explanation: When a parameter name shadows a member variable, `this->memberName` is used to explicitly refer to the member variable of the current object.

q: Can `this` pointer be used inside a static member function?
options: Yes, only if the function is const
No, static functions have no `this` pointer
Yes, but only to access static members
explanation: Static member functions are not associated with any particular object, so they do not have a `this` pointer. Using `this` inside a static function is a compile error.

q: A static data member of a class:
options: Is created for every object of the class
Is shared among all objects of the class
Cannot be accessed outside the class
Is initialized inside the constructor automatically
explanation: A static data member belongs to the class itself, not to individual objects. All objects share the same copy of a static data member.

q: Where must a static data member be defined (outside the class body)?
options: In a source file using scope resolution operator
It does not need a separate definition
explanation: Static data members must be defined outside the class body in a source (.cpp) file using the scope resolution operator: `int ClassName::staticMember = 0;`

q: Which of the following can a static member function access?
options: Only non-static members via a pointer
Only static members of the class
All non-static and static members directly
explanation: Static member functions can only directly access static data members and call other static member functions. They cannot access non-static members without an object reference.

q: Which access specifier makes class members accessible only within the class itself?
explanation: `private` members are accessible only within the class's own member functions and friend functions/classes. They are not accessible from outside the class or derived classes.

q: Which access specifier allows members to be accessible in the class and its derived classes but NOT outside?
explanation: `protected` members are accessible within the class and by derived (child) classes, but not by external code outside the class hierarchy.

q: What is the default access specifier for members of a `class` in C++?
explanation: In C++, the default access specifier for members of a `class` is `private`. (For `struct`, the default is `public`.)

q: A friend function of a class:
options: Is a member function of the class
Can access private and protected members of the class
Must be defined inside the class
explanation: A friend function is declared inside a class with the `friend` keyword but defined outside. It is not a member function but has access to the class's private and protected members.

q: Which keyword is used to declare a friend function inside a class?
explanation: The `friend` keyword is used inside a class declaration to grant a non-member function or another class access to its private and protected members.

q: If class `B` is declared as a friend class of class `A`, then:
options: A can access private members of B
B can access private members of A
A and B can access each other's private members
Neither can access each other's private members
explanation: Friendship is not mutual or transitive. If B is a friend of A, then B's member functions can access A's private members, but A cannot access B's private members unless B also declares A as a friend.

q: What does declaring a member function as `const` signify?
options: The function cannot be called on any object
The function does not modify the object's state
The function's return type is const
The function can only access static members
explanation: A `const` member function guarantees that it will not modify the object's data members. It can be called on both const and non-const objects.

q: Can a `const` object call a non-const member function?
options: Yes, but only if the function is static
No, a const object can only call const member functions
Yes, if the function does not modify data
explanation: A const object can only call member functions declared as `const`. Calling a non-const member function on a const object is a compile-time error.

q: The `mutable` keyword in C++ is used to:
options: Make a variable constant inside a function
Allow a data member to be modified even in a const member function
Declare a variable that can change its type
Prevent a variable from being accessed outside the class
explanation: `mutable` allows a specific data member to be modified even inside a `const` member function, bypassing the const restriction for that member only.

q: What is an inline function?
options: A function whose code is expanded at the call site by the compiler
A function that can only be called once
A function with no return type
explanation: An inline function is a hint to the compiler to replace the function call with the actual function code at the call site, reducing function call overhead. Defined with the `inline` keyword.

q: Member functions defined inside a class body are by default:
explanation: Any member function defined inside the class definition is implicitly treated as an inline function by the compiler.

q: Which of the following is a disadvantage of inline functions?
options: They may increase the size of the compiled code (code bloat)
They do not support recursion warnings from the compiler
explanation: Inlining large functions or frequently called functions can increase the binary size (code bloat) because the function body is duplicated at every call site.

q: How do you declare an array of 5 objects of class `Car`?
explanation: `Car arr[5];` declares an array of 5 Car objects. The default constructor of Car is called for each element. This requires Car to have a default constructor.

q: When an array of objects is created, which constructor is invoked for each element?
explanation: When an array of objects is declared without explicit initialization, the default constructor is called for each element in the array.

q: Shallow copy copies:
options: Only the address of pointer members, not the actual data they point to
The actual data pointed to by pointer members
Only static members of an object
Only public members of an object
explanation: A shallow copy duplicates the object's member values, including pointer values (addresses). Both the original and copy point to the same memory location, which can lead to double-deletion issues.

q: Deep copy copies:
options: The actual data that pointer members point to, allocating new memory
explanation: A deep copy allocates new memory and copies the actual data pointed to by pointer members, so the original and the copy are completely independent of each other.

q: The default copy constructor provided by the compiler performs:
explanation: The compiler-generated copy constructor performs a shallow (memberwise) copy â€” it copies each member's value, including raw pointer addresses, without duplicating heap-allocated data.

q: When is it essential to write a user-defined copy constructor?
options: When the class has no members
When the class contains pointer members pointing to dynamically allocated memory
When the class has only static members
When all members are of primitive types
explanation: When a class manages dynamic memory (raw pointers), the compiler's shallow copy is insufficient. A user-defined copy constructor is needed to perform a deep copy and avoid double-free or dangling pointer issues.

q: What problem arises with shallow copy when objects containing pointers go out of scope?
options: Double deletion of the same memory block
explanation: With shallow copy, both original and copied objects hold the same pointer. When both are destroyed, the destructor attempts to free the same memory twice, causing undefined behavior (double free).

q: In C++, constructor overloading allows a class to have:
options: Only one constructor with default arguments
Multiple constructors with different parameter lists
Constructors with the same name but different return types
A constructor and destructor with the same signature
explanation: Constructor overloading allows defining multiple constructors for a class, each differing in the number or types of parameters, giving flexibility in object creation.

q: What is a delegating constructor in C++11?
options: A constructor that calls a destructor
A constructor that calls another constructor of the same class
A constructor used only in derived classes
explanation: Introduced in C++11, a delegating constructor calls another constructor of the same class in its initializer list, avoiding code duplication across multiple constructors.

q: Which of the following correctly initializes a member variable using a constructor initializer list?
explanation: The constructor initializer list uses a colon followed by member(value) pairs. `MyClass() : x(10) {}` directly initializes `x` to 10, which is more efficient than assignment inside the constructor body.

q: Const member variables in a class MUST be initialized:
options: Inside the constructor body using assignment
explanation: Const data members cannot be assigned after construction. They must be initialized in the constructor's initializer list, since assignment inside the body occurs after construction.

q: What is the order of constructor and destructor calls for two objects `a` and `b` declared in sequence in a local scope?
options: Constructor a, Constructor b, Destructor a, Destructor b
Constructor a, Constructor b, Destructor b, Destructor a
Constructor b, Constructor a, Destructor a, Destructor b
Constructor b, Constructor a, Destructor b, Destructor a
explanation: Objects are constructed in the order of declaration and destroyed in reverse order (LIFO). So: Constructor a â†’ Constructor b â†’ Destructor b â†’ Destructor a.

q: Which of the following is NOT a valid access specifier in C++?
explanation: `internal` is not a C++ access specifier. It exists in C# but not C++. The three C++ access specifiers are `public`, `private`, and `protected`.

q: A class has a private constructor. What is the implication?
options: No objects can ever be created
Objects can only be created by member functions or friend functions of the class
The class cannot be used in programs
The class cannot have any other constructors
explanation: A private constructor prevents external code from directly instantiating the class. Objects can only be created inside the class (e.g., via a static factory method) â€” a common pattern for Singleton design.

q: Which of the following statements about friend classes is TRUE?
options: Friendship is inherited by derived classes
Friendship is neither inherited nor transitive nor mutual by default
explanation: In C++, friendship is not inherited, not transitive, and not mutual. Each relationship must be explicitly declared using the `friend` keyword.

q: Consider: `const MyClass obj;`. Which operations are permitted on `obj`?
explanation: A const object can only have its const member functions called. Any attempt to call a non-const member function or modify its members directly results in a compile error.

q: What does `inline` keyword guarantee?
options: The function will always be inlined by the compiler
The function is a hint to the compiler for inlining, not a guarantee
The function will run faster than normal functions
explanation: `inline` is a suggestion to the compiler, not an absolute directive. The compiler may choose not to inline a function (e.g., if it is too complex or recursive).

q: Which of the following correctly accesses the static member function `count()` of class `Student`?
explanation: Static member functions are called using the class name and scope resolution operator: `Student::count()`. They can also be called via an object, but using the class name is the standard practice.

q: What is the value of `this` inside a static member function?
options: Pointer to the first object created
Static member functions do not have `this`
explanation: Static member functions are not associated with any object, so there is no `this` pointer inside them. Using `this` in a static function is a compile-time error.

q: In C++, an object of a class can be passed to a function:
options: By value, by reference, or by pointer
explanation: In C++, objects can be passed to functions by value (copy is made), by reference (alias), or by pointer. Each approach has different implications for efficiency and modification.

q: When is the copy constructor called? (Select the most complete answer)
options: Only when using the assignment operator
When an object is passed by value to a function, returned by value, or used to initialize another object
Only when explicitly called by the programmer
Only when dynamic memory is involved
explanation: The copy constructor is invoked: (1) when an object is initialized from another object of the same class, (2) when passed by value to a function, and (3) when returned by value from a function.

q: What distinguishes the copy constructor from the assignment operator?
options: Copy constructor is called for already-constructed objects; assignment initializes a new object
Copy constructor initializes a new object; assignment operator copies into an already-existing object
Assignment operator creates a new object; copy constructor modifies existing objects
explanation: Copy constructor creates and initializes a new object from an existing one. The assignment operator (=) copies data into an already-existing (already-constructed) object.

q: Which of the following best describes encapsulation in OOP?
options: Hiding implementation details and exposing only necessary interfaces via access specifiers
Allowing multiple classes to share the same methods
Creating multiple objects from a class
Calling base class functions from derived class
explanation: Encapsulation is the OOP principle of bundling data and methods together and restricting direct access to internal state using access specifiers (private/protected), exposing only what is necessary.

q: What is the output of: `class A { int x; public: A(){x=5;} int getX(){return x;} }; A arr[3]; cout << arr[0].getX();`?
explanation: When `A arr[3]` is declared, the default constructor is called for each of the 3 elements, setting `x=5` for each. So `arr[0].getX()` returns 5.

q: A class with a destructor that frees dynamically allocated memory but uses the default copy constructor is prone to:
options: Shallow copy issues causing double-free and dangling pointers
explanation: The default copy constructor performs a shallow copy of pointer members. When both original and copy objects are destroyed, the destructor frees the same memory twice, causing double-free errors and dangling pointers.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about a destructor?
options: There can be only one destructor per class
It must explicitly be called by the programmer
explanation: A class can have exactly one destructor. It cannot be overloaded (since it takes no parameters), cannot have a return type, and is automatically called when the object's lifetime ends.

q: What is the purpose of `explicit` keyword with a constructor?
options: Prevents implicit type conversions using that constructor
Forces the compiler to always inline the constructor
explanation: The `explicit` keyword prevents the compiler from using a constructor for implicit type conversions or copy-initialization. It ensures the constructor is only called when explicitly invoked.

q: Which type of inheritance does a class exhibit when it inherits from exactly one base class?
explanation: Single inheritance occurs when a derived class inherits from exactly one base class, forming a simple parent-child relationship.

q: In multilevel inheritance, if class C inherits from class B and class B inherits from class A, then class C is:
options: Indirect subclass of A and direct subclass of B
Direct subclass of both A and B
explanation: In multilevel inheritance, class C directly inherits from B (its immediate parent) and indirectly inherits from A through B, making C an indirect subclass of A.

q: The 'diamond problem' in OOP arises in which type of inheritance?
explanation: The diamond problem occurs in multiple inheritance when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from the same base class, creating ambiguity about which version of the base class members to use.

q: Which keyword is used in Java to call the constructor of the parent class from a child class constructor?
explanation: In Java, super() is used inside a child class constructor to explicitly call the parent class constructor. It must be the first statement in the constructor.

q: What is the primary purpose of a virtual base class in C++?
options: To prevent instantiation of a class
To solve the diamond problem by ensuring only one copy of base class members
explanation: A virtual base class in C++ ensures that only one copy of the base class's members is inherited when multiple inheritance creates a diamond-shaped hierarchy, thus resolving the diamond problem.

q: In hierarchical inheritance, which of the following is true?
options: One derived class inherits from multiple base classes
Multiple derived classes inherit from a single base class
A class inherits from a derived class
Two classes inherit from each other
explanation: In hierarchical inheritance, multiple derived classes inherit from a single base class. Example: Dog, Cat, and Bird all inherit from the Animal class.

q: Method overriding in OOP requires that the overriding method in the derived class must have:
options: A different name than the base class method
The same name but different parameters
The same name, same parameters, and same return type
The same name but a different return type
explanation: Method overriding requires the derived class method to have the same name, same parameter list (signature), and the same return type as the method in the base class.

q: Which of the following best describes the 'is-a' relationship in OOP inheritance?
explanation: The 'is-a' relationship represents inheritance. 'A Dog is-a Animal' means Dog inherits from Animal. The 'has-a' relationship (composition) is exemplified by 'A Car has-a Engine'.

q: An abstract class in Java:
options: Can have both abstract and concrete methods
Cannot be extended by another class
explanation: An abstract class in Java can have both abstract methods (without implementation) and concrete methods (with implementation). It cannot be instantiated directly but can be extended.

q: Which keyword is used to declare an abstract method in Java?
explanation: The 'abstract' keyword is used in Java to declare an abstract method. An abstract method has no body and must be implemented by the first concrete subclass.

q: A concrete class in OOP is best defined as:
options: A class that cannot be inherited
A class that implements all abstract methods and can be instantiated
A class with only static methods
explanation: A concrete class is a class that provides implementations for all abstract methods (its own or inherited) and can be instantiated to create objects.

q: In Java, an interface can contain which of the following? (prior to Java 8)
explanation: Prior to Java 8, interfaces could only contain abstract methods and constants (public static final variables). Java 8 introduced default and static methods in interfaces.

q: How does Java handle the diamond problem that arises with multiple inheritance?
options: Java allows multiple class inheritance with virtual keyword
Java does not support multiple class inheritance; uses interfaces instead
Java uses virtual base classes like C++
Java resolves it through method hiding
explanation: Java avoids the diamond problem by not supporting multiple class inheritance. A class can only extend one class but can implement multiple interfaces, which avoids the ambiguity issue.

q: Constructor chaining refers to:
options: Calling a method from a constructor
One constructor calling another constructor in the same or parent class
Defining multiple constructors with different parameters
explanation: Constructor chaining is the process where one constructor calls another constructor â€” either in the same class using this() or in the parent class using super(). It ensures proper initialization.

q: Which of the following represents hybrid inheritance?
options: A class inheriting from one class only
A combination of two or more types of inheritance
Multiple classes inheriting from one base class
A class inheriting through multiple levels
explanation: Hybrid inheritance is a combination of two or more types of inheritance (e.g., combining hierarchical and multiple inheritance). It can lead to the diamond problem in languages that support multiple inheritance.

q: In Java, which statement about the 'super' keyword is correct?
options: super can be used to access private members of parent class
super() must always be explicitly called in every constructor
super refers to the immediate parent class object
super can only be used in static methods
explanation: The 'super' keyword in Java refers to the immediate parent class. It can be used to access parent class methods, variables, and constructors. It cannot access private members.

q: What happens when a subclass does NOT override an abstract method of its abstract parent class?
options: The method is automatically implemented as empty
explanation: If a subclass does not implement all abstract methods of its abstract parent class, the subclass itself must also be declared abstract. Otherwise, a compile-time error occurs.

q: The 'has-a' relationship in OOP is implemented through:
explanation: The 'has-a' relationship is implemented through composition (where one class contains an instance of another class as a member variable), as opposed to 'is-a' which is implemented through inheritance.

q: In C++, to declare a virtual base class, which syntax is used?
explanation: In C++, virtual inheritance is specified in the derived class declaration: 'class B : virtual A' or 'class B : public virtual A'. This ensures only one copy of A exists in the hierarchy.

q: Which of the following is NOT a valid type of inheritance in OOP?
explanation: Cyclic inheritance (where class A inherits from B and B inherits from A) is not valid in OOP as it creates an infinite loop and is not allowed by any object-oriented programming language.

q: When a derived class method has the same signature as a base class method, this is called:
explanation: When a derived class provides a specific implementation for a method already defined in its base class with the same name and signature, it is called method overriding. This enables runtime polymorphism.

q: In Java, which access modifier prevents a method from being overridden in a subclass?
explanation: The 'final' keyword prevents method overriding in Java. A final method cannot be overridden in any subclass. Static and private methods are also not overridden (they are hidden/not inherited).

q: What is the output concept when super() is not explicitly called in a subclass constructor in Java?
options: The parent's default constructor is called implicitly
The object is created without calling any parent constructor
explanation: If super() is not explicitly called in a subclass constructor, Java automatically inserts a call to the parent's no-argument (default) constructor as the first statement. If the parent has no default constructor, a compile error occurs.

q: A class that implements an interface must:
options: Implement all abstract methods declared in the interface
Extend the interface using extends keyword
Declare all interface variables as instance variables
explanation: A concrete class that implements an interface must provide implementations for all abstract methods declared in the interface. If it doesn't, the class must be declared abstract.

q: In multilevel inheritance A â†’ B â†’ C, when an object of C is created, the order of constructor calls is:
explanation: In multilevel inheritance, constructors are called from the topmost base class down to the derived class. So for A â†’ B â†’ C, the order is A(), then B(), then C().

q: Which OOP concept does the following best illustrate: 'A SavingsAccount is-a BankAccount'?
explanation: The 'is-a' relationship directly represents inheritance. SavingsAccount inheriting from BankAccount means SavingsAccount is a specialized type of BankAccount.

q: In Python, which built-in function is used to check if a class is a subclass of another?
explanation: issubclass(SubClass, ParentClass) returns True if SubClass is a subclass of ParentClass. isinstance() checks if an object is an instance of a class or its subclasses.

q: Multiple inheritance in Python is resolved using:
options: Method Resolution Order (MRO) using C3 linearization
explanation: Python uses the C3 linearization algorithm to determine the Method Resolution Order (MRO) in multiple inheritance scenarios. The MRO can be viewed using ClassName.__mro__ or ClassName.mro().

q: Which of the following statements about abstract methods is TRUE?
options: Abstract methods have a complete implementation
Abstract methods can be defined in a concrete class
Abstract methods must be overridden by the first concrete subclass
explanation: Abstract methods have no implementation in the class where they are declared. They must be overridden and fully implemented by the first concrete (non-abstract) subclass in the inheritance hierarchy.

q: Which type of inheritance is represented when class Dog and class Cat both inherit from class Animal?
explanation: Hierarchical inheritance occurs when multiple classes (Dog, Cat) inherit from a single base class (Animal). Each derived class has its own relationship with the common parent.

q: In Java, an interface extending another interface uses which keyword?
explanation: In Java, an interface can extend another interface using the 'extends' keyword (not 'implements'). A class uses 'implements' to implement an interface.

q: The 'override' annotation (@Override) in Java is used to:
options: Force the compiler to create an overridden version
Indicate to the compiler that the method is intended to override a parent method
Prevent subclasses from further overriding the method
explanation: @Override annotation tells the compiler that the method is intended to override a method in the superclass. If the method doesn't actually override anything, the compiler generates an error, helping catch bugs.

q: What is true about constructors and inheritance in Java?
options: Constructors are not inherited but can be invoked using super()
Constructors cannot be called from subclasses
Only default constructors can be called using super()
explanation: Constructors are NOT inherited in Java. However, a subclass can call a parent class constructor using super() to ensure proper initialization of inherited members.

q: In C++, the diamond problem can be completely resolved by declaring the common base class as:
explanation: In C++, declaring the base class as virtual in intermediate classes (virtual inheritance) ensures that only one copy of the base class members exists in the final derived class, resolving the diamond problem.

q: Which relationship is preferred over inheritance in OOP design when reusing functionality?
explanation: The principle 'favor composition over inheritance' is a key OOP design guideline. Composition (has-a) provides more flexibility, reduces coupling, and avoids inheritance hierarchy complexity.

q: What is the term for a class that provides partial implementation and is meant to be subclassed?
explanation: An abstract class provides partial implementation â€” it can have both implemented (concrete) and unimplemented (abstract) methods. It serves as a template for subclasses to complete the implementation.

q: In Java, a class can implement multiple interfaces because:
options: Interfaces only contain method signatures, so there's no implementation conflict
The JVM resolves conflicts at runtime
Interfaces are treated as abstract classes
explanation: Java allows implementing multiple interfaces because interfaces (before Java 8) only contain method signatures without implementation, so there is no ambiguity about which method body to use â€” the implementing class provides the body.

q: Which of the following is an example of multilevel inheritance?
options: class B extends A; class C extends A
class B extends A; class C extends B
class A extends B; class B extends A
explanation: Multilevel inheritance forms a chain: class B extends A, and class C extends B creates a three-level chain A â†’ B â†’ C. Option A is hierarchical, option C is multiple inheritance, option D is cyclic (invalid).

q: When using super keyword to call a parent method, it accesses:
options: The method in the topmost class of the hierarchy
The method in the immediate parent class
Any overridden version in the hierarchy
Only static methods of the parent
explanation: The super keyword always refers to the immediate parent class. super.methodName() calls the method in the direct parent class, not any higher-level ancestor.

q: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of an abstract class?
options: It can have both abstract and concrete methods
explanation: An abstract class CANNOT be instantiated directly. You cannot create an object of an abstract class using the 'new' keyword. All other options are valid characteristics of abstract classes.

q: In the context of OOP inheritance, what does 'base class' mean?
options: The lowest class in the hierarchy
The class from which other classes are derived
The class with the most members
The class that implements the most interfaces
explanation: A base class (also called parent class or superclass) is the class from which other classes are derived. The derived class (child class or subclass) inherits properties and behaviors from the base class.

q: Method overriding is an example of which OOP principle?
explanation: Method overriding is a key mechanism for achieving runtime polymorphism (dynamic polymorphism). It allows a subclass object to call its own version of a method even when referenced through a parent class reference.

q: Which statement about multiple inheritance in Java is correct?
options: Java supports multiple inheritance through classes
Java supports multiple inheritance through interfaces only
Java does not support any form of multiple inheritance
Java supports multiple inheritance through abstract classes
explanation: Java does not support multiple inheritance through classes (to avoid the diamond problem), but it does support multiple inheritance through interfaces. A class can implement multiple interfaces.

q: What is constructor chaining using 'this()' in Java?
options: Calling a method from the constructor
One constructor calling another constructor within the same class
explanation: Constructor chaining using this() allows one constructor in a class to call another constructor in the same class. this() must be the first statement and is used to avoid code duplication among overloaded constructors.

q: If class B extends class A, and both have a method display(), calling display() on a B object will invoke:
options: B's display() due to method overriding
Both A's and B's display() simultaneously
Neither â€” it causes a compile error
explanation: When B overrides display() from A, and you call display() on a B object, B's version is invoked due to runtime polymorphism (dynamic method dispatch). The overriding method takes precedence.

q: A pure virtual function in C++ is equivalent to which concept in Java?
explanation: A pure virtual function in C++ (declared as 'virtual void func() = 0') has no implementation in the base class and must be overridden in derived classes â€” exactly like an abstract method in Java.

q: Which of the following access modifiers allows a member to be accessible in subclasses but not outside the class hierarchy?
explanation: The 'protected' access modifier makes a member accessible within the same class, subclasses (even in different packages), and classes in the same package. It enables controlled inheritance-based access.

q: In Java, which of the following is true about interface default methods (introduced in Java 8)?
options: They must be overridden by implementing classes
They provide a default implementation and can optionally be overridden
They can only be called on the interface name
explanation: Java 8 introduced default methods in interfaces that provide a concrete implementation. Implementing classes can use the default implementation or override it. This was added for backward compatibility.

q: The concept of 'is-a' relationship in OOP is formally known as:
explanation: The 'is-a' relationship represents generalization/specialization through inheritance. The subclass (specialization) is a more specific type of the superclass (generalization). E.g., Car is a Vehicle.

q: Which of the following correctly describes hybrid inheritance?
options: Combination of more than one type of inheritance in a single program
A class inheriting from an interface and a class simultaneously
Inheritance limited to three levels only
explanation: Hybrid inheritance is a mix of two or more inheritance types (e.g., single + multiple, or hierarchical + multilevel) in a single inheritance hierarchy. It can lead to complexity and the diamond problem.

q: Which type of polymorphism is resolved at compile time?
explanation: Compile-time polymorphism (static polymorphism) is resolved at compile time. It includes function overloading and operator overloading. The compiler decides which function to call based on the number and types of arguments.

q: Function overloading in C++ is an example of:
explanation: Function overloading is a compile-time polymorphism mechanism. The compiler resolves which overloaded function to call based on the function signature (number and types of parameters) at compile time.

q: Which keyword is used to achieve runtime polymorphism in C++?
explanation: The 'virtual' keyword is used to achieve runtime polymorphism in C++. When a function is declared virtual in a base class, the call is resolved at runtime using the vtable mechanism.

q: What is a vtable?
options: A runtime table of virtual function pointers maintained per class
A table of virtual base classes
explanation: A vtable (virtual table) is a per-class table containing pointers to the virtual functions of that class. Each object of a class with virtual functions has a hidden pointer (vptr) that points to the class's vtable, enabling runtime dispatch.

q: What does vptr stand for in C++ polymorphism?
explanation: vptr stands for 'virtual pointer'. It is a hidden pointer added by the compiler to each object of a class that has virtual functions. The vptr points to the vtable of the actual (dynamic) type of the object.

q: A pure virtual function in C++ is declared as:
explanation: A pure virtual function is declared by appending '= 0' to the function declaration: 'virtual void func() = 0;'. A class containing at least one pure virtual function becomes an abstract class.

q: A class that contains at least one pure virtual function is called:
explanation: A class with at least one pure virtual function is called an abstract class. Objects of an abstract class cannot be instantiated directly. Derived classes must override all pure virtual functions to be concrete (instantiable).

q: Can you instantiate an abstract class directly in C++?
options: Yes, only if it has a constructor
explanation: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly. Attempting to create an object of an abstract class results in a compile-time error. You can, however, have pointers or references of abstract class type pointing to derived class objects.

q: Which of the following correctly describes operator overloading?
options: Defining new operators not in C++
Giving new meaning to existing operators for user-defined types
Using operators for pointer arithmetic only
explanation: Operator overloading allows existing C++ operators to be given new meaning (behavior) when applied to user-defined types (classes/structs). It is a form of compile-time polymorphism.

q: Upcasting in C++ means:
options: Casting a base class pointer to a derived class pointer
Casting a derived class pointer to a base class pointer
Casting a pointer to an integer
explanation: Upcasting is the implicit conversion of a derived class pointer/reference to a base class pointer/reference. It is always safe and doesn't require an explicit cast. It is used to achieve polymorphic behavior.

q: Downcasting in C++ means:
options: Casting a derived class pointer to a base class pointer
Casting a base class pointer to a derived class pointer
Casting an integer to a pointer
Converting a class to a primitive type
explanation: Downcasting is converting a base class pointer/reference to a derived class pointer/reference. It must be done explicitly and can be unsafe. dynamic_cast should be used for safe downcasting with runtime type checking.

q: Which cast operator should be used for safe downcasting in C++?
explanation: dynamic_cast is used for safe downcasting. It performs a runtime type check. If the cast is invalid (the object is not of the target type), dynamic_cast returns nullptr (for pointers) or throws std::bad_cast (for references).

q: What does dynamic_cast return if the cast fails (for pointers)?
explanation: When dynamic_cast fails for a pointer cast, it returns nullptr. This allows the programmer to check if the cast succeeded before using the pointer. For reference casts, it throws std::bad_cast on failure.

q: RTTI stands for:
explanation: RTTI stands for Runtime Type Information. It is a C++ mechanism that provides information about an object's type at runtime. RTTI supports dynamic_cast and typeid operators.

q: Which operator is used to obtain type information at runtime in C++?
explanation: The typeid operator returns a reference to a std::type_info object that represents the type of an expression. It is used with RTTI to get runtime type information.

q: Static binding (early binding) means:
options: Function call resolved at compile time
Function call resolved by the linker only
Function call resolved dynamically via vtable
explanation: Static binding (early binding) means the function call is resolved at compile time. Non-virtual function calls, overloaded functions, and inline functions use static binding. The compiler directly embeds the function address.

q: Dynamic binding (late binding) means:
options: Function call resolved at compile time
Function call resolved at link time
Function call resolved at runtime via vtable
explanation: Dynamic binding (late binding) means the function call is resolved at runtime. In C++, virtual functions use dynamic binding via the vtable mechanism. The actual function called depends on the runtime type of the object.

q: Which of the following functions uses dynamic binding in C++?
explanation: Virtual member functions use dynamic binding. When a virtual function is called through a base class pointer or reference, the vtable is consulted at runtime to dispatch to the correct overridden function in the derived class.
Since show() is declared virtual in Base, dynamic binding is used. The vptr of the Derived object points to Derived's vtable, so Derived::show() is called, printing 'Derived'.
Since show() is NOT virtual in Base, static binding is used. The function called is determined by the pointer type (Base*), not the actual object type. So Base::show() is called, printing 'Base'.

q: How many vtables does a class hierarchy with one base and two derived classes typically have?
explanation: Each class with virtual functions gets its own vtable. So a hierarchy with Base, Derived1, and Derived2 will have three vtables â€” one for Base and one for each derived class, each containing pointers to their respective virtual function implementations.

q: Which of the following operators CANNOT be overloaded in C++?
explanation: The scope resolution operator '::' cannot be overloaded in C++. Other operators that cannot be overloaded include '.', '.*', '?:', and 'sizeof'. The '<<' (stream insertion), '+', and '[]' operators can all be overloaded.

q: In C++, which of the following is true about pure virtual functions?
options: They can optionally have a body
They are automatically inherited as virtual
explanation: Pure virtual functions CAN optionally have a body (implementation) in C++. However, the class remains abstract. The derived class still must override the pure virtual function to be concrete, but the base class can provide a default implementation callable via explicit scope.

q: The 'override' keyword in C++11 is used to:
options: Explicitly indicate that a function overrides a virtual function
Prevent further overriding of a function
explanation: The 'override' keyword explicitly indicates that a function is intended to override a virtual function in the base class. If the function does not actually override a base class virtual function, the compiler generates an error, preventing subtle bugs.

q: The 'final' keyword in C++11 applied to a virtual function means:
options: The function cannot be overridden in derived classes
The function is the last virtual function in the vtable
explanation: The 'final' keyword prevents a virtual function from being overridden in further derived classes. It can also be applied to a class to prevent the class from being inherited.

q: What is the size overhead introduced by having a vtable in a class?
options: One pointer (vptr) added to each object
One pointer per virtual function per object
explanation: Each object of a class with virtual functions contains one hidden vptr (virtual pointer), typically the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes). All objects of the same class share one vtable; the overhead per object is just one pointer.

q: Which of the following is NOT a feature of abstract classes in C++?
options: They can have non-virtual member functions
explanation: Abstract classes cannot be directly instantiated â€” that is the defining restriction. However, they CAN have constructors (called by derived class constructors), non-virtual member functions, data members, and even implemented virtual functions.

q: In C++, which function call mechanism uses the vtable?
options: Calling a non-virtual function through an object
Calling a virtual function through a base class pointer
explanation: When a virtual function is called through a base class pointer or reference, the runtime uses the vptr of the actual object to look up the vtable and dispatch to the correct overriding function. This is runtime polymorphism via vtable.

q: What is the result of applying typeid to a non-polymorphic class object?
explanation: For non-polymorphic types (no virtual functions), typeid returns the static compile-time type of the expression. For polymorphic types, typeid can return the dynamic type if applied through a pointer or reference.

q: Which header file provides the std::type_info class used by typeid?
explanation: The std::type_info class is declared in the <typeinfo> header. The typeid operator returns a const reference to a std::type_info object, which has methods like name() and before() for type comparison.

q: In function overloading, which of the following is NOT used to differentiate overloaded functions?
explanation: Return type alone cannot differentiate overloaded functions in C++. Overloaded functions must differ in the number of parameters, the types of parameters, or the order of parameter types. Differing only in return type causes a compile error.

q: What is object slicing in C++?
options: Splitting an object into multiple sub-objects
Loss of derived class data when a derived object is assigned to a base class object by value
Deleting part of an object's memory
explanation: Object slicing occurs when a derived class object is assigned to a base class object by value. The derived-class-specific members are 'sliced off', and only the base class portion is copied. Polymorphism is lost.

q: When should a base class destructor be declared virtual?
options: When the class has any virtual functions or is intended to be a base class with derived objects deleted via base pointer
Only when the class is abstract
explanation: A base class destructor should be virtual when objects of derived classes will be deleted through base class pointers. Without a virtual destructor, only the base class destructor is called, causing resource leaks in the derived class.

q: What happens if you delete a derived object through a base class pointer when the base class destructor is NOT virtual?
options: Derived class destructor is called correctly
Only the base class destructor is called â€” undefined behavior / resource leak
explanation: Without a virtual destructor in the base class, deleting a derived object through a base class pointer results in undefined behavior. Typically only the base class destructor runs, causing the derived class portion's resources to leak.

q: Which of the following is an example of runtime polymorphism?
explanation: Virtual function overriding is runtime polymorphism. The decision of which function to call is made at runtime based on the actual type of the object (via vtable). Function overloading, operator overloading, and templates are compile-time mechanisms.

q: In C++, which of the following is true about static_cast vs dynamic_cast for downcasting?
options: static_cast is safer because it checks at runtime
dynamic_cast is safer because it checks at runtime
explanation: dynamic_cast is safer for downcasting because it performs a runtime type check. If the cast is invalid, it returns nullptr (for pointers) or throws std::bad_cast (for references). static_cast performs no runtime check and can produce undefined behavior if the cast is invalid.

q: A class with all pure virtual functions is most analogous to which concept in Java?
explanation: A C++ class with all pure virtual functions and no data members is analogous to a Java interface. It defines a contract (set of methods) that concrete derived classes must implement, without providing any implementation itself.

q: What is the primary purpose of the vtable in C++?
options: To support runtime dispatch of virtual function calls
explanation: The vtable's primary purpose is to support runtime dispatch of virtual function calls. When a virtual function is called via a base class pointer, the runtime looks up the vtable (through the vptr) to find and call the correct overriding function.

q:
Consider: class A { public: virtual void f(){} }; class B: public A {}; class C: public B {};
If B does not override f(), which vtable entry does C's vtable use for f()?
explanation: Since B does not override f(), B's vtable entry for f() points to A::f. C inherits from B and also does not override f(), so C's vtable entry for f() also points to A::f (inherited through B).

q: Which of the following correctly overloads the '+' operator for a class Point?
options: Point operator+(Point a, Point b) as member function with two parameters
Point operator+(const Point& other) as member function
explanation: As a member function, the '+' operator takes one explicit parameter (the right-hand operand); the left-hand operand is the implicit 'this' object. The signature 'Point operator+(const Point& other)' is correct.

q: Which of the following statements about function overloading is FALSE?
options: Overloaded functions can have different return types
Overloaded functions must have the same name
Overloaded functions must differ in parameter list
Two functions differing only in return type are NOT considered overloaded
explanation: While overloaded functions CAN have different return types, that alone is not sufficient to distinguish them. Two functions with the same name and parameter list but different return types cause a compile error. The statement 'overloaded functions can have different return types' is misleading â€” they MUST differ in parameter list.

q: What does the typeid().name() function return?
options: The memory address of the type
An implementation-defined string representing the type name
explanation: typeid().name() returns an implementation-defined null-terminated character string representing the type name. The exact string is compiler-specific (e.g., MSVC may return 'class Foo', GCC may return mangled names).

q: In C++, can constructors be declared virtual?
options: Yes, but only in abstract classes
Yes, using the virtual keyword before the constructor
explanation: Constructors cannot be virtual in C++. When a constructor runs, the object's vtable pointer (vptr) is being set up, so virtual dispatch isn't meaningful or possible at that stage. Virtual destructors are common, but virtual constructors do not exist in C++.

q: Which of the following is true about a derived class that does not override a base class's pure virtual function?
options: The derived class also becomes abstract
A default implementation from the base class is used
explanation: If a derived class does not override all pure virtual functions from its base class(es), the derived class itself becomes an abstract class and cannot be instantiated. This is how the contract is enforced.

q: Which type of cast would you use to add or remove the const qualifier from a variable?
explanation: const_cast is used to add or remove the const (or volatile) qualifier from a variable. It is the only C++ cast that can change the constness of a variable.

q: In the context of RTTI, dynamic_cast requires the base class to be:
options: Polymorphic (have at least one virtual function)
explanation: dynamic_cast requires the base class to be polymorphic, meaning it must have at least one virtual function (commonly the virtual destructor). Without this, dynamic_cast cannot perform runtime type checking and will fail to compile.

q: What is early binding?
options: Binding that occurs when a program starts
Binding where function addresses are resolved at compile time
explanation: Early binding (static binding) means function addresses are resolved at compile time. The compiler directly inserts the function address in the call instruction. Non-virtual functions use early binding.

q: What is the behavior when dynamic_cast is used on a reference and the cast fails?
explanation: When dynamic_cast is applied to a reference (not a pointer) and the cast fails, it throws std::bad_cast (defined in <typeinfo>). For pointers, failure returns nullptr. References cannot be null, hence the exception.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of compile-time polymorphism over runtime polymorphism?
options: Better performance due to no vtable overhead
explanation: Compile-time polymorphism (function overloading, templates) avoids the vtable/vptr overhead of runtime polymorphism. Function calls are resolved directly by the compiler, leading to better performance due to elimination of indirect dispatch.

q: In multiple inheritance, if a class derives from two base classes that both have virtual functions, how many vptrs does the derived object typically contain?
options: One vptr per base class with virtual functions
No vptr â€” multiple inheritance disables vtables
One vptr only if both bases share a common vtable
explanation: In multiple inheritance, when two base classes each have virtual functions (separate vtables), the derived object typically contains one vptr per base class. Each vptr points to the respective adjusted vtable for that part of the object layout.

q: Which of the following best describes the Liskov Substitution Principle in the context of polymorphism?
options: A derived class object must be substitutable for a base class object without altering correctness
A base class object can always substitute a derived class object
Virtual functions must be overridden in all derived classes
Abstract classes must implement all inherited methods
explanation: The Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) states that objects of a derived class must be substitutable for objects of the base class without altering the correctness of the program. It underpins runtime polymorphism â€” base class pointers/references should work correctly with any derived class object.

q: In Java, which block is guaranteed to execute whether or not an exception is thrown?
explanation: The finally block always executes after the try/catch blocks, regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught, making it ideal for cleanup code.

q: Which of the following is the root class of all exceptions in Java?
explanation: Throwable is the root class of Java's exception hierarchy. Both Error and Exception extend Throwable.

q: What is a checked exception in Java?
options: An exception that is checked at runtime
An exception that must be declared or handled at compile time
An exception thrown by the JVM only
explanation: Checked exceptions must be either caught or declared using the 'throws' keyword. The compiler enforces this at compile time.

q: Which of the following is an unchecked exception in Java?
explanation: NullPointerException extends RuntimeException, which is unchecked. The compiler does not require it to be declared or caught.

q: What keyword is used to manually throw an exception in Java?
explanation: 'throw' is used to explicitly throw an exception object. 'throws' is used in method signatures to declare that a method may throw an exception.

q: Which keyword in a method signature indicates the method may throw a checked exception?
explanation: The 'throws' keyword in a method declaration indicates that the method may throw the listed checked exceptions, alerting the caller to handle them.

q: What happens when an exception is thrown and not caught in any method on the call stack?
options: The program terminates and the JVM prints the stack trace
The exception is automatically handled by the OS
explanation: If an exception propagates all the way up the call stack without being caught, the JVM terminates the thread and prints the exception message and stack trace.

q: In multiple catch blocks, how should exception types be ordered?
explanation: More specific exceptions must be caught before more general ones. Placing a parent class catch block before a child class catch block causes a compile-time error in Java.

q: What is the output behavior when a finally block has a return statement and the try block also has a return statement?
options: The try block's return value is used
The finally block's return value overrides the try block's return value
explanation: If the finally block contains a return statement, it overrides any return statement in the try or catch blocks, which can lead to unexpected behavior.

q: Which of the following correctly re-throws an exception in Java?
explanation: To re-throw a caught exception, you use the 'throw' keyword followed by the exception reference within the catch block.

q: How do you create a custom exception in Java?
options: Extend the Exception class or one of its subclasses
Declare a class with the keyword 'exception'
explanation: Custom exceptions are created by extending Exception (for checked) or RuntimeException (for unchecked) or any appropriate subclass.

q: What is 'exception propagation'?
options: Converting one exception type to another
The process of an unhandled exception moving up the call stack looking for a handler
Catching an exception and rethrowing a different one
explanation: When an exception is thrown and not caught in the current method, it propagates to the calling method. This continues until a handler is found or the stack is exhausted.

q: In C++, what is 'stack unwinding'?
options: Expanding the call stack to allow more function calls
The process of destroying local objects when an exception propagates through stack frames
Optimizing tail calls in recursive functions
